ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for oatmeal cookie with fruit; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single oatmeal fruit cookie weighs approximately 30 g, providing around 139 kcal, 5.7 g fat, and 7.1 g sugar per serving.

nutrient_source.title: Oatmeal Cookie (Fruit) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Oatmeal cookie (fruit), cr_id F11080041, with confidence 0.95. All per-100g macros and micronutrients are sourced from this record. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy item 18159 (oatmeal cookie) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry.

gi_source.title: Oatmeal cookie GI — University of Sydney GI Database (analogous estimate: oatmeal cookie, GI ~55)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI value of 55 is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for oatmeal cookies as the closest analogous food. Per the University of Sydney GI classification scale, GI 55 or below is classified as 'low' (low: ≤55, medium: 56–69, high: ≥70). Glycemic load per 30 g serving calculated as (55 × 19.63 g available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 ≈ 10.8. The input record cited GI 55 from an embedded GI database consistent with the University of Sydney reference range for oatmeal cookies.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium (316 mg/100g), high sugar (23.52 g/100g), and estimated saturated fat (~7.5 g/100g) collectively elevate cardiovascular and blood-pressure risk.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should strictly limit oatmeal fruit cookies due to 316 mg sodium per 100 g, high added sugar driving insulin-mediated sodium retention, and saturated fat exceeding the 5 g/100g threshold; a single 30 g cookie contributes approximately 95 mg sodium and should be an occasional treat only.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar at 23.52 g/100g far exceeds the 15 g/100g diabetes threshold, and total carbohydrate is 65.42 g/100g; despite 4.76 g dietary fibre, the high sugar and refined flour content cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit oatmeal fruit cookies because the 23.52 g/100g sugar content and GI of 55 produce a meaningful glycemic load (~10.8 per 30 g serving); plain rolled oats with fresh fruit is a substantially lower-GI alternative.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat ~7.5 g/100g and trans fat ~0.2 g/100g from butter or shortening, combined with high sugar (23.52 g/100g) and sodium (316 mg/100g), present a multi-pathway cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals managing cardiovascular disease should treat oatmeal fruit cookies as an occasional indulgence only, given the combination of saturated fat (~7.5 g/100g), trans fat (~0.2 g/100g), high sodium (316 mg/100g), and high added sugar (23.52 g/100g), all of which adversely affect lipid profiles and endothelial function.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Estimated trans fat ~0.2 g/100g from butter or shortening is directly pro-inflammatory; saturated fat ~7.5 g/100g and sugar 23.52 g/100g further amplify pro-inflammatory cytokine activity.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid oatmeal fruit cookies because even small amounts of trans fat (~0.2 g/100g) are strongly pro-inflammatory, and the high sugar and saturated fat content further promote inflammatory mediators.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Oatmeal Cookie (Fruit) is a baked sweet biscuit made primarily from rolled oats, dried fruits (such as raisins, cranberries, or sultanas), wheat flour, sugar, butter or shortening, and eggs. The cookie is typically soft to chewy in texture with a mildly sweet, wholesome oat flavour complemented by the natural tartness and sweetness of the dried fruit. Per 100 g, it provides 462 kcal, 7.7 g protein, 18.87 g fat (with an estimated 7.5 g saturated fat and ~0.2 g trans fat from butter or shortening), 65.42 g carbohydrate, 23.52 g sugar, 4.76 g dietary fibre, 316 mg sodium, 155 mg calcium, and 1.33 mg iron. Despite the presence of oats — a wholegrain ingredient associated with beta-glucan and modest cholesterol-lowering effects — the high sugar content (23.52 g/100g), elevated sodium (316 mg/100g), and significant saturated fat make this a high-concern food for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis. The glycemic index is approximately 55 (low by University of Sydney classification, where low is ≤55), yielding a glycemic load of roughly 10.8 per standard 30 g serving. Oatmeal fruit cookies are widely consumed as a snack or breakfast accompaniment in Western and international food cultures and are commonly found in Singapore supermarkets and cafes. Clinically, they should be treated as an occasional treat rather than a regular dietary component for any health-conscious individual.
common_user_queries: How many calories in an oatmeal cookie?
Is oatmeal cookie high in sugar?
Is oatmeal cookie good for weight loss?
Can I eat oatmeal cookies if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F11080041) with a recorded confidence of 0.95. The food entry is canonical, reviewed, and matches an exact HPB record. GI of 55 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database analogous oatmeal cookie entry, consistent with the input record's embedded GI value.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this oatmeal fruit cookie is not separately reported in the HPB Foodo database entry F11080041 or in USDA FoodData Central for the closest equivalent as of 2026. potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g set to -1: these micronutrient and moisture values are not reported in the HPB source record for this item. trans_fat_g estimated at 0.2 g/100g based on typical butter or shortening content in commercial oatmeal cookies; this is an estimate and not directly reported by HPB. saturated_fat_g estimated at 7.5 g/100g from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy analogous oatmeal cookie entry as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this record. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for oatmeal cookies; per the University of Sydney classification scale, GI ≤55 is classified as 'low' (low: ≤55, medium: 56–69, high: ≥70), therefore gi_category is set to 'low'. The exact fruit-enriched variant has not been independently tested. This food is not a Singaporean dish; multilingual local-name fields (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) are not applicable and are set to null. The food is classified as ultra-processed due to the combination of refined flour, added sugar, shortening, and commercial baking agents.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 3: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' to comply with the University of Sydney GI classification scale, where GI ≤55 = low, GI 56–69 = medium, GI ≥70 = high. A GI of exactly 55 falls at the upper boundary of the 'low' category. Updated glycemic_load_per_serving from 12.0 to 10.8 (calculated as 55 × 19.63 g available carbohydrate per 30 g serving / 100). Updated gi_source notes, llm_training description, and notes field to reflect the corrected GI category. This resolves the q4_glycemic_completeness failure identified by the validator in iteration 2.
